He made a pitcher of iced tea, y;pr^^^''. He put everything in a basket. 55 Toadhurried backto the river. 'Trog,"heshouted, ''it'sme. It's your best friend, Toad!" Frog was too farawayto hear. Toad took offhis jacket and waved ithkea flag. Frogwastoo far away to see. Toadshouted and waved, but it wasno use. 56 Frog sat on the island. He did not see or hear Toad 57 A turtle swam by. Toad climbed on the turtle's back. "Turtle," said Toad, <.(. carry meto the island Frog is there. He wants to be alone." 58 "If Frog wants to be alone," said the turtle, ''why don't you leave him alone?" ''Maybe you are right," said Toad. "Maybe Frog does not want to see me. Maybe he does not want me to be his friend anymore." "Yes, maybe," said the turtle as he swam to the island. 59 "Frog!" cried Toad. 'T amsorry forall the dumb thingsIdo. I am sorry forall the silly thingsI say. Pleasebe my friend again!" Toad slipped offthe turtle. With a splash, he fellin theriver. ^ ! ' 4 c-^ ,X'-o^ f , ^^'-''if-.i^- Frog pulled Toad up onto the island. Toad looked in the basket. The sandwiches were wet. The pitcher of iced tea was empty. ''Our lunch is spoiled," said Toad. "I made it for you, Frog, so that you would be happy. 95 61 "But Toad,"saidFrog. "I am happy. Iamveryhappy. This morning when I woke up I fehgood because thesun wasshining. Ifelt good because Iwas a frog. AndIfeh good because Ihave you for a friend. Iwantedtobe alone. Iwantedto thinkabout howfine everything is." 62 "Oh," said Toad. "I guess that is a very good reason for wanting to be alone." "Now," said Frog, "I will be glad not to be alone. Let's eat lunch." 63 Frog and Toad stayed on the island all afternoon. They ate wet sandwiches without icedtea. They were two close friends sittingalone together. 64 AnICan Read Book™ Meet two of the most beloved characters in all of children's literature!
